label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example because it is a primary Randomized Clinical Trial (RCT) that uses a smartphone app and a Reinforcement Learning (AI) algorithm to deliver a real-world lifestyle intervention. CONTENT: An AI-Powered Lifestyle Intervention vs Human Coaching in the Diabetes Prevention Program. The study compares an AI-led intervention (via mobile app and digital scale) to human coaching. The AI used a reinforcement learning algorithm to personalize messaging, successfully achieving weight loss and physical activity goals in adults with prediabetes.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example because it proposes a "Just-In-Time Adaptive Intervention" (JITAI) using a digital assistant on a smartphone. It utilizes Machine Learning (contextual bandits) and NLP to interact with users in the real world. CONTENT: Increasing physical activity using a just-in-time adaptive digital assistant supported by machine learning. The paper describes a system with three components: educational conversations via NLP, a nudge engine using reinforcement learning for hyper-personalized cues based on real-time data (GPS, weather), and a generative AI Q&A feature.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example describing a protocol for an RCT of an app-based digital assistant ("MoveMentor") that employs Large Language Models (LLMs) and Reinforcement Learning for personalized coaching. CONTENT: MoveMentor—examining the effectiveness of a machine learning and app-based digital assistant to increase physical activity. The intervention uses an app to provide educational conversations, just-in-time nudges, and chat-based Q&A, adapting in real-time to user data to increase physical activity.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example because it details the design of a smartphone application that uses empirical data mining to create a self-learning module for timing physical activity reminders. CONTENT: The Design and Development of a Personalized Leisure Time Physical Activity Application. The study develops the "PAUL" app based on behavior change theories and data mining of historical running data. It features a self-learning module to optimize the timing of reminders and feedback, demonstrating a direct application of AI principles in a user-facing mobile tool.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example as it uses Computer Vision (AI) on a smartphone to assess physical activity quality in adults. CONTENT: PocketCoach: Real-time Squat Analysis using Smartphone Computer Vision. We propose a mobile application that uses the phone's camera and a lightweight convolutional neural network (CNN) to detect user pose and provide corrective feedback during exercise sessions in urban parks.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example featuring an LSTM (Deep Learning) model integrated into a smartphone app to predict sedentary bouts. CONTENT: Predicting Sedentary Behavior in Office Workers using Recurrent Neural Networks on Mobile Devices. This study implements a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model directly on a smartphone to analyze accelerometer streams and predict prolonged sitting, triggering just-in-time notifications to walk.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example using a Recommender System (AI) for walking routes in a city setting. CONTENT: CityWalk: A Context-Aware Recommender System for Urban Walking Routes. The app uses collaborative filtering to suggest personalized walking paths based on user preferences, current weather, and noise levels derived from urban sensing data.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example of a Generative AI chatbot acting as a health coach for adults. CONTENT: GenFit: Evaluating a Large Language Model-based Physical Activity Coach. We conducted a 4-week pilot study where adults interacted with a fine-tuned GPT-based chatbot on their phones. The AI coach provided motivational support and answered fitness queries, increasing daily step counts.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example involving Reinforcement Learning (Contextual Bandits) for optimizing notification timing. CONTENT: Optimizing Walking Prompts via Bandit Algorithms: A Micro-Randomized Trial. The study tests a smartphone app that uses a contextual bandit algorithm to learn the optimal time of day to send walking suggestions to users, adapting to their calendar and location.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example using Natural Language Processing (NLP) to analyze digital diary entries for PA motivation. CONTENT: Sentiment Analysis of Physical Activity Diaries for Tailored Interventions. A smartphone app allows users to log their daily activity experiences via voice. An NLP module analyzes sentiment to categorize motivation levels and adjust the intensity of weekly activity goals.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example of a Gamified App using AI for difficulty adjustment. CONTENT: AI-Driven Difficulty Adjustment in an Active Commuting Game. The application uses a machine learning classifier to assess user fatigue from GPS and accelerometer data, dynamically adjusting the game's point rewards to encourage sustained active transport without burnout.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example using Clustering (Unsupervised Learning) to phenotype users for tailored interventions. CONTENT: Identifying Walker Types in Urban Environments using Unsupervised Learning. We analyzed smartphone GPS traces from 500 adults using K-means clustering to identify distinct mobility patterns, which then fed into a rule-based app to deliver tailored route suggestions.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example of a System Architecture paper describing an mHealth AI framework. CONTENT: Architecture for a Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning Framework in mHealth. We present the design of a smartphone-based physical activity monitoring system that uses Federated Learning to train activity recognition models locally on user devices, preserving privacy while improving accuracy.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example of a Feasibility Study using machine learning for activity recognition. CONTENT: Feasibility of On-Device Random Forest Classifiers for Activity Recognition in Older Adults. This study tests the battery efficiency and accuracy of a Random Forest algorithm running locally on a smartphone to detect walking, sitting, and standing in an elderly population.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example of a JITAI using Bayesian Networks. CONTENT: A Bayesian Network Approach to Just-in-Time Adaptive Interventions for Walking. The app constructs a probabilistic model of the user's receptivity to notifications, using Bayesian inference to decide whether to send a "Move Now" prompt.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example of AI-driven social support. CONTENT: SocialFit: AI-Matching for Physical Activity Partners. The smartphone application uses a matching algorithm based on user fitness levels, schedule, and location to pair adults for joint exercise sessions in urban centers.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example using Computer Vision for environment assessment. CONTENT: Mapping Walkability: Crowdsourcing Sidewalk Quality via Smartphone Cameras. Users record their walks, and a deep learning model analyzes video frames to score sidewalk quality, providing this data back to users to encourage walking on safer routes.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example of a predictive model for dropout prevention. CONTENT: Predicting Attrition in mHealth Exercise Programs using Survival Analysis and Random Forests. The study uses interaction data from a smartphone app to predict when a user is likely to stop using the app, triggering automated re-engagement messages.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example of combining Wearables and Smartphones with AI. CONTENT: Multi-modal Sensor Fusion for Activity Intensity Estimation. A smartphone app aggregates data from a smartwatch and the phone's internal sensors, using a neural network to estimate energy expenditure more accurately than single-device solutions.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example of a protocol for an AI intervention. CONTENT: Protocol for a SMART Trial of an AI-based Walking App. This paper outlines the methodology for a Sequential Multiple Assignment Randomized Trial (SMART) testing different reinforcement learning reward functions in a smartphone walking app.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example using Fuzzy Logic (AI) for goal setting. CONTENT: Adaptive Step Goals using Fuzzy Logic Control. The smartphone app uses a fuzzy logic controller to set daily step goals that are challenging yet attainable, based on the user's past week of performance.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example of Chatbot-delivered CBT for exercise adherence. CONTENT: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Exercise via an AI Chatbot. The study evaluates a text-based conversational agent on a smartphone that uses decision trees and NLP to deliver CBT techniques to overcome barriers to exercise.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example using Anomaly Detection for health alerts. CONTENT: Detecting Sudden Decreases in Mobility in Seniors. An unsupervised anomaly detection algorithm runs on the smartphone to identify significant drops in daily steps, alerting the user and suggesting gentle recovery activities.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example of a hybrid AI system (Expert System + ML). CONTENT: Hybrid AI for Personalized Workout Plans. The app combines expert knowledge rules for safety with machine learning for preference adaptation to generate daily workout routines for users in home settings.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example of Green AI / Sustainability in mHealth. CONTENT: Energy-Efficient Deep Learning for Continuous Activity Monitoring. We propose a model compression technique (pruning) that allows complex activity recognition networks to run on smartphones with minimal battery impact, promoting sustainable long-term monitoring.

label: NEG
text: REASONING: This is a negative example because it is a Scoping Review of existing methodologies, not a primary empirical study testing a specific intervention. CONTENT: A scoping review of methodologies for applying artificial intelligence to physical activity interventions. The review aggregates 24 studies on ML, DL, and RL in physical activity but does not present a new experimental intervention or primary data collection.

label: NEG
text: REASONING: This is a negative example because it utilizes Agent-Based Modeling (ABM) for simulation rather than a direct smartphone-based intervention with real-world users. CONTENT: Leveraging agent-based modeling and a randomized intervention to advance childhood physical activity. The protocol describes creating an ABM to simulate complex systems and effect pathways in childhood physical activity, using the model to extrapolate strategies rather than testing an AI app directly on users.

label: NEG
text: REASONING: This is a negative example because it focuses on developing a conceptual model to inform simulations (Agent-Based Modeling), lacking a direct AI-driven app component for real-time user intervention. CONTENT: Developing a Conceptual Model for Collective Patterns and Leisure-Time Physical Activity Inequalities. The paper aims to capture dynamic interactions to conceptualize and inform an agent-based model for simulating physical activity inequalities, which is a theoretical/modeling task rather than an mHealth application.

label: NEG
text: REASONING: This is a negative example because it focuses on validating behavioral models for simulation using automated knowledge extraction, falling under the excluded category of simulation/ABM studies. CONTENT: Validating and constructing behavioral models for simulation and projection using automated knowledge extraction. The paper proposes a method to extract evidence into a knowledge graph to construct or validate Agent-Based Models (ABM), focusing on simulation methodology rather than real-world physical activity promotion via smartphones.

label: NEG
text: REASONING: This is a negative example because the population is children (under 18). CONTENT: Gamified Physical Activity for Primary School Children using Machine Learning. This study evaluates an app designed for children aged 6-12 to promote recess activity. Although it uses AI, the target population is excluded from this review.

label: NEG
text: REASONING: This is a negative example because it is a Review article. CONTENT: Systematic Review of Smartphone Apps for Physical Activity. We searched five databases to identify commercial apps that promote walking. This paper summarizes existing literature and does not present original empirical data or a new system.

label: NEG
text: REASONING: This is a negative example because it lacks an AI component (Rule-based only). CONTENT: Efficacy of SMS Reminders for Walking: A Randomized Trial. Participants received daily text messages at 9 AM reminding them to walk. The intervention used a fixed schedule without any machine learning, adaptivity, or intelligence.

label: NEG
text: REASONING: This is a negative example because it does not involve a smartphone (Wearable only). CONTENT: Accuracy of Fitbit Charge 5 for Step Counting in Seniors. The study validates the step-counting algorithm of a wrist-worn device. The data was downloaded via USB to a PC; no smartphone app was used as an interface or intervention tool.

label: NEG

label: NEG
